Home   Sun, Moon & Space   Eclipses   August 27–28, 2026 Partial Lunar Eclipse

August 27–28, 2026 Partial Lunar Eclipse

Eclipse InformationEclipse Map3D Globe MapLive Stream

This is a very deep (96.2%) partial lunar eclipse visible to residents of North and South America and parts of Europe, and Africa.

Watch the eclipse LIVE!

This eclipse is visible in Wheaton - go to local timings and animation

What This Lunar Eclipse Looks Like

The curvature of the shadow's path and the apparent rotation of the Moon's disk is due to the Earth's rotation.

Where to See the Eclipse

Try our new interactive eclipse maps. Zoom in and search for accurate eclipse times and visualizations for any location.

Regions seeing, at least, some parts of the eclipse: Europe, West in Asia, Africa, North America, South America, Pacific, Atlantic, Indian Ocean, Antarctica.

Expand for a list of selected cities where the partial eclipse is visible
  • Budapest, Hungary
  • Vienna, Vienna, Austria
  • Lagos, Lagos, Nigeria
  • Santiago, Chile
  • Amsterdam, Netherlands
  • Paris, Paris, France
  • Caracas, Venezuela
  • Rome, Italy
  • Lima, Lima, Peru
  • Mexico City, Ciudad de México, Mexico
  • Montréal, Quebec, Canada
  • Brussels, Brussels, Belgium
  • San Salvador, El Salvador
  • Lisbon, Lisbon, Portugal
  • Havana, Cuba
  • Casablanca, Morocco
  • Johannesburg, South Africa
  • Dublin, Ireland
  • New York, New York, USA
  • Algiers, Algeria
  • Madrid, Madrid, Spain
  • Washington DC, District of Columbia, USA
  • Rio de Janeiro, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
  • London, England, United Kingdom
  • Buenos Aires, Argentina
  • Guatemala City, Guatemala
  • Santo Domingo, Dominican Republic
  • Berlin, Berlin, Germany
  • Zagreb, Croatia
  • Prague, Czechia

This eclipse is visible in Wheaton - go to local timings and animation

Eclipse Map and Animation

When the Eclipse Happens Worldwide — Timeline

Lunar eclipses can be visible from everywhere on the night side of the Earth, if the sky is clear. From some places the entire eclipse will be visible, while in other areas the Moon will rise or set during the eclipse.

Eclipse Stages WorldwideUTC TimeLocal Time in Wheaton*Visible in Wheaton
Penumbral Eclipse beginsAug 28 at 01:23:58Aug 27 at 8:23:58 pmYes
Partial Eclipse beginsAug 28 at 02:33:54Aug 27 at 9:33:54 pmYes
Maximum EclipseAug 28 at 04:12:53Aug 27 at 11:12:53 pmYes
Partial Eclipse endsAug 28 at 05:51:59Aug 28 at 12:51:59 amYes
Penumbral Eclipse endsAug 28 at 07:01:47Aug 28 at 2:01:47 amYes

* The Moon is above the horizon during this eclipse, so with good weather conditions in Wheaton, the entire eclipse is visible.

Quick Facts About This Eclipse

DataValueComments
Magnitude0.930Fraction of the Moon’s diameter covered by Earth’s umbra
Obscuration96.2%Percentage of the Moon's area covered by Earth's umbra
Penumbral magnitude1.965Fraction of the Moon's diameter covered by Earth's penumbra
Overall duration5 hours, 38 minutesPeriod between the beginning and end of all eclipse phases
Duration of partial phase3 hours, 18 minutesPeriod between the beginning and end of the partial phase
Duration of penumbral phases2 hours, 20 minutesCombined period of both penumbral phases

Eclipse calculations usually accurate to a few seconds

How Many People Can See This Eclipse?

Number of People Seeing...Number of People*Fraction of World Population
At least some of the penumbral phase3,600,000,00044.41%
At least some of the partial phase3,340,000,00041.21%
All of partial phase1,280,000,00015.78%
The entire eclipse from beginning to end987,000,00012.16%

* The number of people refers to the resident population (as a round number) in areas where the eclipse is visible. timeanddate has calculated these numbers using raw population data provided by the Center for International Earth Science Information Network (CIESIN) at Columbia University. The raw data is based on population estimates from the year 2000 to 2020.

An Eclipse Never Comes Alone!

A solar eclipse always occurs about two weeks before or after a lunar eclipse.

Usually, there are two eclipses in a row, but other times, there are three during the same eclipse season.

All eclipses 1900 — 2199

This is the second eclipse this season.

First eclipse this season: August 12, 2026 — Total Solar Eclipse

How accurate is the eclipse calculation

Partial Lunar Eclipse Countdown

Aug 28, 2026 at 01:23:58 UTC

Lunar Eclipse Live Show

We are streaming the total lunar eclipse on Mar 2–3 LIVE

Eclipses in 2026

  • Feb 17, 2026 – Annular Solar Eclipse
  • Mar 2–3, 2026 — Total Lunar Eclipse
  • Aug 12, 2026 – Total Solar Eclipse
  • Aug 27–28, 2026 — Partial Lunar Eclipse (this page)

  • Feb 6, 2027 – Annular Solar Eclipse
  • Feb 20–21, 2027 — Penumbral Lunar Eclipse
  • Jul 18–19, 2027 — Almost Lunar Eclipse
  • Aug 2, 2027 – Total Solar Eclipse
  • Aug 16–17, 2027 — Penumbral Lunar Eclipse
